subject: Geography
trap_factor: Students often confuse surface freshwater bodies (lakes/rivers) as the largest store, not realising groundwater vastly exceeds them. The word 'remaining' after ice caps is key.
elimination_hint: Recall the hydrological cycle: after glaciers, groundwater (~30% of freshwater) far exceeds lakes/rivers (<1%) and atmospheric moisture (<1%).
factual_hooks: Groundwater is ~30% of total freshwater
Glaciers/ice caps hold ~69% of all freshwater
Lakes/rivers hold <1% of freshwater
contrast_with: Surface water vs groundwater storage volumes
contemporary_relevance: Groundwater depletion is a critical global water security challenge.

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Loktak Lake is in Manipur, not associated with Barail Range; Namdapha is in Arunachal Pradesh near Patkai hills, not Dafla Hills — these subtle mismatches trap unprepared students.
elimination_hint: Confirm only Pair 1: Nokrek Biosphere Reserve is indeed in Meghalaya's Garo Hills. Pairs 2 and 3 have wrong geographical associations.
factual_hooks: Nokrek BR is in Garo Hills, Meghalaya
Loktak Lake is in Manipur (not Barail Range)
Namdapha NP is in Arunachal Pradesh near Patkai/Mishmi hills
contrast_with: Barail Range is in Assam/Manipur border — different from Garo Hills
contemporary_relevance: Northeast India's biodiversity hotspots are conservation priorities.

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Students may exclude revolution of earth thinking it only changes seasons, not surface dynamics — but tidal effects and orbital mechanics do influence geomorphology.
elimination_hint: All six forces contribute to dynamic changes: electromagnetic radiation drives weathering, geothermal drives volcanism, gravity causes erosion, plate movements cause tectonics, rotation causes Coriolis, revolution affects climate seasonality.
factual_hooks: All six forces are responsible for dynamic changes on Earth's surface
Geothermal energy drives volcanism and plate tectonics
Earth's rotation causes Coriolis effect influencing erosion patterns
contrast_with: Endogenic vs exogenic forces on Earth's surface
contemporary_relevance: Understanding dynamic earth processes is critical for disaster risk reduction.

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Zonal Councils are statutory bodies under States Reorganisation Act, not constitutional — students often confuse statutory with constitutional bodies. NDC and Planning Commission were both extra-constitutional.
elimination_hint: None of the three — NDC, Planning Commission, Zonal Councils — find mention in the Constitution; all are statutory or executive creations.
factual_hooks: NDC was an extra-constitutional body (now dissolved)
Planning Commission was not in Constitution (replaced by NITI Aayog)
Zonal Councils are statutory under States Reorganisation Act 1956
contrast_with: Inter-State Council (constitutional, Art. 263) vs Zonal Councils (statutory)
contemporary_relevance: Distinction between constitutional and extra-constitutional bodies remains relevant after dissolution of Planning Commission.

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Students may pick option B (sunlight/nutrients) but grasslands actually have adequate nutrients; the real limiters are water stress and periodic fires that kill tree seedlings.
elimination_hint: Ecological succession in grasslands is arrested by water scarcity (trees need more water than grasses) and fire events that burn tree seedlings but grasses regenerate from roots.
factual_hooks: Fire is a key ecological factor maintaining grassland biome
Grasses have deep root systems allowing regeneration after fire
Trees require more water than grasses, limiting colonisation in semi-arid grasslands
contrast_with: Forest vs grassland succession dynamics
contemporary_relevance: Grassland conservation is critical as they are among the most threatened biomes globally.

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Oceans are tempting as the most productive due to their size, but per-unit-area productivity is low. Mangroves have highest productivity per unit area, followed by grasslands, then lakes, then open oceans.
elimination_hint: Think in terms of net primary productivity per unit area: Mangroves > Grasslands > Lakes > Oceans (open ocean is least productive per unit area).
factual_hooks: Mangroves have highest net primary productivity among ecosystems
Open ocean has lowest NPP per unit area despite its size
Wetlands and estuaries are highly productive ecosystems
contrast_with: Total global productivity (oceans dominate due to area) vs per-area productivity
contemporary_relevance: Mangrove conservation is vital given their high ecological productivity and role in carbon sequestration.

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Justice Krishna Iyer is widely associated with PIL but he was never Chief Justice of India — he was a Supreme Court judge who pioneered PIL jurisprudence.
elimination_hint: Statement 1 is factually wrong — Krishna Iyer was a puisne judge of Supreme Court, not CJI. Statement 2 is correct; eliminate options containing 1 as correct.
factual_hooks: Justice V.R. Krishna Iyer was a Supreme Court judge, not CJI
He pioneered PIL jurisprudence in India along with Justice P.N. Bhagwati
PIL began in India in late 1970s-80s
contrast_with: CJI vs puisne judge of Supreme Court
contemporary_relevance: PIL remains a powerful tool for judicial activism and citizen access to justice.

subject: Geography
trap_factor: All three options sound plausible as soil conservation methods; students may pick hilly/slope areas for contour bunding without knowing the correct answer is 'none of the above' since contour bunding is used on slopes/hills, not the listed terrains.
elimination_hint: Contour bunding is used on sloping lands in semi-arid regions to prevent runoff and erosion — none of options A, B, C describe this correctly, making D the answer.
factual_hooks: Contour bunding creates ridges along contour lines on sloping terrain
It reduces surface runoff and soil erosion on slopes
Not used in flat plains, desert margins, or scrublands as described in options
contrast_with: Contour bunding vs strip cropping vs check dams
contemporary_relevance: Soil conservation techniques are essential for sustainable agriculture in rain-fed areas.

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Creating 'autonomous regions in tribal areas' sounds like a PESA objective but it is not — PESA focuses on self-governance within existing Panchayat framework, not creating separate autonomous regions.
elimination_hint: PESA's objectives are self-governance, recognition of traditional rights, and protection from exploitation — 'autonomous regions' was a deliberate misleading option.
factual_hooks: PESA 1996 extended Panchayati Raj to Schedule V areas
It recognises traditional tribal governance (gram sabha powers)
PESA does not create autonomous regions — that is under 6th Schedule
contrast_with: PESA (5th Schedule) vs Autonomous District Councils (6th Schedule)
contemporary_relevance: PESA implementation remains critical for tribal rights and forest governance.

subject: Science & Technology
factual_hooks: Bose-Einstein Statistics applies to bosons (integer spin particles)
S.N. Bose's work with Einstein led to prediction of Bose-Einstein Condensate
Fifth state of matter (BEC) was experimentally confirmed in 1995
contrast_with: Bose-Einstein Statistics vs Fermi-Dirac Statistics
mains_intro: Satyendra Nath Bose's collaboration with Einstein produced a statistical framework that fundamentally changed our understanding of quantum particles.
mains_body_points: Bose-Einstein Statistics governs the behaviour of bosons (particles with integer spin like photons, Higgs boson)
Unlike classical particles, bosons can occupy the same quantum state — leading to concept of Bose-Einstein Condensate
The statistics explained blackbody radiation, Planck's law derivation, and laser coherence
BEC experimentally confirmed in 1995 by Cornell and Wieman, winning 2001 Nobel Prize
Practical applications: lasers, superfluidity, superconductivity, precision measurement
mains_conclusion: Bose's contribution stands as one of India's greatest gifts to fundamental physics, with applications ranging from lasers to quantum computing.
contemporary_relevance: Bose-Einstein Condensate research underpins quantum computing and next-generation precision instruments.

subject: Governance
trap_factor: The name 'Ebraham Alkazi' may be unfamiliar; students might guess medicine or engineering. He is a landmark figure in Indian theatre/drama.
elimination_hint: Ebrahim Alkazi is renowned as one of India's greatest theatre directors who transformed Indian theatre — associate him with National School of Drama.
factual_hooks: Ebrahim Alkazi directed the National School of Drama for nearly two decades
He revolutionised Indian theatre with his productions
He was awarded Padma Vibhushan for contribution to arts

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Students may think PSL covers only agriculture, missing that it includes MSMEs and weaker sections — all three are included, making D the correct answer.
elimination_hint: RBI's PSL framework mandates banks to lend to multiple sectors including agriculture, micro/small enterprises, and weaker sections — answer is 'all of the above'.
factual_hooks: Priority Sector Lending covers agriculture, MSMEs, weaker sections, housing, education
40% of Adjusted Net Bank Credit (ANBC) must go to priority sectors
Agriculture alone gets 18% of ANBC under PSL norms
contrast_with: Priority sector vs non-priority sector lending
contemporary_relevance: PSL norms are being revised to include renewable energy and social infrastructure sectors.

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Textiles and paper/pulp industries also use large volumes of water, making the question tricky. Thermal power plants use water for cooling and are the largest industrial consumers.
elimination_hint: Thermal power plants require enormous water for steam generation and cooling towers, far exceeding other industries — this is a well-established fact in Indian industrial geography.
factual_hooks: Thermal power is the largest industrial consumer of water in India
Water is used for cooling in thermal plants (once-through or recirculating)
Water stress from thermal plants is a growing environmental concern
contrast_with: Thermal vs hydroelectric water use
contemporary_relevance: Water stress from thermal power plants is increasingly discussed in context of climate change.

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Social security schemes and reducing IMR are important goals but don't directly 'harvest' the demographic dividend — skill development is the correct route to converting young population into productive workforce.
elimination_hint: Demographic dividend requires productive employment of the young workforce — skill development enables this by making youth employable; other options address welfare, not productivity.
factual_hooks: India has the youngest population globally with a median age ~28 years
Demographic dividend requires skill development to be productive
Window of demographic dividend closes around 2040-2055 for India
contrast_with: Demographic dividend vs demographic burden
contemporary_relevance: India's demographic dividend window is narrowing — skilling urgency has risen sharply.

subject: History
trap_factor: Options B, C, D describe other aspects of dance/drama (mudras, abhinaya, shringara) — Tribhanga specifically refers to the three-bend body posture, not facial expression or storytelling.
elimination_hint: The term Tribhanga literally means 'three bends' — one leg bent, waist curved opposite, neck curved again — a characteristic S-shape pose in Indian classical sculpture and dance.
factual_hooks: Tribhanga means 'three bends' — at neck, waist, and knee
Common in Indian sculpture from Mathura and Gupta periods
Used in Odissi dance and depicted on temple sculptures
contrast_with: Tribhanga vs Samabhanga (erect pose) vs Atibhanga (extreme bend)

subject: History
trap_factor: Statement 2 is a trap — Annie Besant was president of the Theosophical Society but did not found it; it was founded by Helena Blavatsky and Henry Steel Olcott in 1875.
elimination_hint: Eliminate statement 2 (Besant joined, didn't found the Theosophical Society). Statements 1 and 3 are correct — Home Rule Movement and INC Presidency (1917 Calcutta session).
factual_hooks: Annie Besant founded the Home Rule League in 1916
She was President of INC in 1917 (Calcutta session)
Theosophical Society was founded by Blavatsky and Olcott in 1875; Besant became its president later
related_concepts: Bal Gangadhar Tilak's Home Rule League
Moderate vs Extremist divide in INC
contrast_with: Annie Besant's Home Rule League vs Tilak's Home Rule League

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Students may exclude option 3 (effective demand increase) thinking it's only a Keynesian concept not directly linked to inflation, but demand-pull inflation is a standard mechanism.
elimination_hint: All three cause inflation: excess money supply (monetary), reduced output (supply-side), and increased demand (demand-pull) — answer is D.
factual_hooks: Three inflation types: demand-pull, cost-push, monetary
Increase in money supply causes inflation (quantity theory of money)
Decrease in output with same demand raises prices (supply shock inflation)
contrast_with: Inflation vs deflation vs stagflation
contemporary_relevance: Inflation targeting is the cornerstone of RBI's monetary policy framework since 2016.

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Foreign loans are a trap — they are not part of foreign exchange reserves (reserves are what you have, not what you owe). World Bank loans are liabilities, not reserves.
elimination_hint: India's forex reserves = Foreign currency assets + Gold holdings of RBI + SDRs + Reserve tranche position in IMF — loans from foreign countries/World Bank are NOT included.
factual_hooks: India's forex reserves comprise: FCAs, gold, SDRs, and RTP in IMF
SDRs (Special Drawing Rights) are allocated by IMF and form part of reserves
Foreign loans are liabilities, not assets — not part of reserves
contrast_with: Assets (forex reserves) vs liabilities (foreign loans)
contemporary_relevance: India's forex reserves crossed $600 billion in recent years, providing import cover security.

subject: History
trap_factor: Bimbavati Devi is associated with Manipuri dance — students may confuse northeastern classical forms, especially between Manipuri and Sattriya.
elimination_hint: Bimbavati Devi is a noted exponent of Manipuri dance; associate northeastern Indian states with their classical dance forms to eliminate other options.
factual_hooks: Manipuri dance originated in Manipur, associated with Vaishnavism
Bimbavati Devi is a celebrated Manipuri dancer
Manipuri is one of 8 classical dance forms recognised by Sangeet Natak Akademi
contrast_with: Manipuri vs Sattriya (Assam)

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Students may pick option C (borrowing from public) as most inflationary, but borrowing from the public merely transfers existing money — it doesn't create new money. Only creating new money directly increases money supply.
elimination_hint: Creating new money (deficit monetisation) is most inflationary as it directly expands money supply without corresponding output increase; public borrowing is least inflationary as it doesn't create new money.
factual_hooks: Deficit monetisation (printing money) is most inflationary
Public borrowing crowds out private investment but doesn't create new money
Bank borrowing partly creates money through credit multiplier
contrast_with: Monetised deficit vs non-monetised deficit
contemporary_relevance: India abolished automatic monetisation of deficit in 1997 under an agreement with RBI.

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Students confuse money demand increase with price effects — when demand for money rises (people want to hold more cash), interest rate rises, not falls.
elimination_hint: Liquidity preference theory: higher demand for money with fixed supply → interest rate rises to equilibrate money market.
factual_hooks: Higher money demand with fixed supply raises interest rates
Interest rate is the 'price' of money/credit
Keynes's liquidity preference theory explains this relationship
contrast_with: Money market vs goods market equilibrium
contemporary_relevance: RBI's repo rate changes directly influence money market interest rates.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Students may pick CO2 concentration thinking modified atmosphere storage is the reason — but the primary reason for cold storage longevity is reduced respiration rate due to low temperature.
elimination_hint: Cold temperature directly slows down enzymatic activity and cellular respiration in fruits, reducing ripening and spoilage — this is the primary mechanism.
factual_hooks: Cold storage reduces rate of respiration, slowing ripening
Low temperature inhibits ethylene production (ripening hormone)
Modified atmosphere storage (CO2 increase) is a secondary technique
contrast_with: Cold storage vs modified atmosphere storage
contemporary_relevance: Cold chain infrastructure is critical for reducing India's massive post-harvest food losses.

subject: History
trap_factor: Bal Gangadhar Tilak is more famous for criticising moderates, so students may not recall that Aurobindo Ghosh wrote 'New Lamps for Old' as a young critic of the INC's moderate approach.
elimination_hint: Aurobindo Ghosh wrote 'New Lamps for Old' in the Indu Prakash (1893) criticising the INC's moderate politics before he became a revolutionary and spiritual leader.
factual_hooks: Aurobindo Ghosh wrote 'New Lamps for Old' in 1893 in Indu Prakash
He criticised the moderate Congress leadership's petitioning approach
Later Aurobindo became a key leader of the Extremist faction
prerequisite_concepts: Moderate vs Extremist politics in INC
contrast_with: Moderate approach (petitions) vs Extremist approach (mass agitation)

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Students may think some of these species are only vulnerable, not endangered — all three (Gharial, Leatherback turtle, Swamp deer) are indeed classified as endangered or critically endangered.
elimination_hint: Gharial is Critically Endangered, Leatherback turtle is Vulnerable to Critically Endangered, Swamp deer (Barasingha) is Vulnerable — all qualify as endangered species broadly.
factual_hooks: Gharial is Critically Endangered — one of India's most threatened reptiles
Leatherback turtle nests on Indian coasts and is threatened by pollution
Swamp deer (Barasingha) is found in Kanha NP and is Vulnerable
contrast_with: Critically Endangered vs Endangered vs Vulnerable on IUCN scale
contemporary_relevance: Gharial conservation in Chambal River is a flagship wildlife protection effort.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Students confuse 'reduces friction by reducing actual area' with 'increases area' — ball bearings reduce friction by minimising the effective contact area and converting sliding friction to rolling friction.
elimination_hint: Ball bearings convert sliding friction (high) to rolling friction (low) and reduce the effective contact area between surfaces — option C is correct.
factual_hooks: Ball bearings reduce friction by converting sliding to rolling friction
Effective contact area is reduced, decreasing friction force
Rolling friction coefficient is much lower than sliding friction
contrast_with: Sliding friction vs rolling friction

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Students may pick only refraction or only dispersion — rainbow requires all three: dispersion (splitting white light), refraction (bending at water surface), and internal reflection (reflecting inside droplet).
elimination_hint: All three phenomena — dispersion, refraction, and internal reflection — work together inside a water droplet to produce a rainbow. Answer is D.
factual_hooks: Rainbow formation involves refraction at entry, internal reflection inside, and dispersion
Different wavelengths of light refract at different angles causing colour separation
Secondary rainbow forms due to two internal reflections
contrast_with: Reflection vs refraction vs dispersion

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: Given the year (2007), students may confuse with Russia or USA joint exercises — 'Hand-in-Hand' is specifically the India-China bilateral military exercise.
elimination_hint: 'Hand-in-Hand' is the dedicated India-China joint military exercise series that began in 2007 focusing on counter-terrorism operations.
factual_hooks: Hand-in-Hand is India-China joint military exercise started in 2007
The exercise focuses on counter-terrorism and humanitarian assistance
It alternates between India and China
contrast_with: Hand-in-Hand (India-China) vs Shakti (India-France) vs Indra (India-Russia)
contemporary_relevance: India-China military exercises have been impacted by border tensions since 2020.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Students may pick option A (unfavourable soil minerals) — but the real reason is mechanical damage to root hairs during transplantation, which affects water and nutrient absorption.
elimination_hint: Root hairs are delicate structures responsible for water and mineral absorption; their loss during transplantation disrupts the seedling's ability to survive — option C.
factual_hooks: Root hairs are primary site of water and mineral absorption in plants
Transplantation physically damages root hairs
Wilting after transplantation is due to water stress from root hair loss
contrast_with: Primary root vs root hairs in function

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Technical progress in the world economy and trade growth help but don't necessarily cause growth in country X; population growth can actually hinder per capita growth — only capital formation is a necessary condition.
elimination_hint: Capital formation (investment) is the only option that necessarily leads to economic growth in country X by expanding productive capacity. External factors (world trade, global tech) help but aren't necessary conditions.
factual_hooks: Capital formation is the core driver of economic growth (Harrod-Domar model)
Population growth can reduce per capita income if not matched by output growth
World trade growth benefits open economies but isn't a necessary condition for country X
contrast_with: Necessary vs sufficient conditions for economic growth
contemporary_relevance: India's investment-to-GDP ratio is a key determinant of its growth trajectory.

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Students may guess 90th or 91st Amendment; the 92nd Amendment Act 2003 added Bodo, Dogri, Maithili, and Santhali to the Eighth Schedule raising it from 18 to 22 languages.
elimination_hint: 91st Amendment dealt with anti-defection; 93rd dealt with OBC reservations in education — the 92nd Amendment is specifically the one that added four languages to the 8th Schedule.
factual_hooks: 92nd Constitutional Amendment Act 2003 added Bodo, Dogri, Maithili, Santhali
Raised Eighth Schedule languages from 18 to 22
Eighth Schedule currently has 22 recognised languages
contrast_with: Scheduled languages vs classical languages vs official language
contemporary_relevance: Demands for inclusion of more languages in the 8th Schedule continue.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Statements 2 and 3 are both wrong: viruses cannot be cultured in synthetic media (need host cells), and viruses can be transmitted by non-biological vectors (physical contact, fomites, air). Only statement 1 is correct.
elimination_hint: Viruses are obligate intracellular parasites — they lack metabolic enzymes for energy generation (only statement 1 true). They must use host cell machinery and can spread without biological vectors.
factual_hooks: Viruses have no metabolic enzymes; they hijack host cell machinery
Viruses can only be cultured in living host cells/embryos, not synthetic media
Viruses can spread via physical contact, air, water — not only biological vectors
contrast_with: Virus vs bacteria in terms of reproduction and metabolic activity
contemporary_relevance: Understanding viral biology is fundamental to vaccine and antiviral drug development.

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Students may pick options 1 and 2 only, not knowing thorns in cacti are modified leaves — thorns/spines in desert plants are indeed modified leaves that reduce surface area for transpiration.
elimination_hint: All three are correct leaf modifications in desert plants: waxy hard leaves reduce evaporation, tiny/absent leaves reduce surface area, and thorns are modified leaves that reduce water loss. Answer is D.
factual_hooks: Cacti thorns are modified leaves (phyllodes)
Hard waxy cuticle reduces transpiration in xerophytes
Reduced leaf surface minimises water loss in desert plants
contrast_with: Xerophytes vs hydrophytes vs mesophytes

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Gravity appears strongest in everyday life but is actually the weakest of the four fundamental forces — strong nuclear force is the strongest. Option A is the incorrect statement.
elimination_hint: Gravity is paradoxically the weakest of four fundamental forces despite its cosmic dominance — it acts over infinite range with no screening. Strong nuclear force is ~10^38 times stronger than gravity.
factual_hooks: Strong nuclear force is the strongest (holds protons/neutrons in nucleus)
Gravity is the weakest fundamental force
Weak nuclear force causes beta decay (radioactivity)
Electromagnetism acts only on charged particles
contrast_with: Relative strengths: Strong > Electromagnetic > Weak > Gravity
contemporary_relevance: Unification of all four forces is the ultimate goal of theoretical physics.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Statement 2 (teleportation of matter) is a sci-fi extrapolation not supported by Higgs boson discovery; statement 3 (better nuclear fuels) is also incorrect — Higgs explains mass, not nuclear binding energy.
elimination_hint: Higgs boson explains why particles have mass — only statement 1 is correct. Statements 2 and 3 are scientifically unsupported extrapolations.
factual_hooks: Higgs boson ('God particle') was discovered at CERN in 2012
It explains why elementary particles have mass through interaction with Higgs field
Its discovery validated the Standard Model of particle physics
contrast_with: Mass-giving Higgs mechanism vs gravity's effect on mass
contemporary_relevance: Higgs boson discovery at CERN validated decades of theoretical particle physics research.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Students may pick only option 1 (drought resistance) — mycorrhiza also helps with pH tolerance and disease resistance, making all three correct.
elimination_hint: Mycorrhizal fungi form symbiotic associations with plant roots extending absorptive area, providing drought tolerance, pH buffering, and pathogen resistance — all three statements are correct.
factual_hooks: Mycorrhizae extend root absorptive surface area dramatically
They help plants tolerate pH extremes and drought conditions
Used in bioremediation and rehabilitation of degraded land
contrast_with: Mycorrhiza vs rhizobium symbiosis
contemporary_relevance: Mycorrhizal biotechnology is emerging as a sustainable alternative to chemical fertilisers.

subject: Governance
trap_factor: The Finance Commission chairman is NOT a member of NDC — students confuse NDC with other bodies. NDC comprised PM, Union Cabinet Ministers, Chief Ministers of States, and Lt. Governors of UTs.
elimination_hint: NDC = PM + Union Cabinet Ministers + Chief Ministers of States (and Lt. Governors/Administrators of UTs). Finance Commission Chairman was not a member.
factual_hooks: National Development Council was dissolved and replaced by NITI Aayog Governing Council in 2014
NDC comprised PM, all Union Ministers, all CMs, and UT Lt. Governors
Finance Commission Chairman was not a constituent member of NDC
contrast_with: NDC vs Inter-State Council vs Finance Commission
contemporary_relevance: NITI Aayog's Governing Council now performs many functions of the erstwhile NDC.

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Statement 1 says 450 members but the Constitution prescribes maximum 500 members for a State Legislative Assembly — 450 is incorrect. Statement 2 (minimum age 25) is correct.
elimination_hint: Article 170 prescribes maximum 500 (not 450) members for Vidhan Sabha; minimum age is 25 years (Art. 173). Only statement 2 is correct.
factual_hooks: Article 170: State Legislative Assembly has 60-500 members
Minimum age to contest Vidhan Sabha elections is 25 years
Direct elections from territorial constituencies as per Article 170
contrast_with: Lok Sabha (Article 81, max 530+20) vs Vidhan Sabha (Article 170, max 500)

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Option A ('produced by nationals') sounds correct but national income is the money value of final goods and services — option A conflates NNP with GNP without clarity on factor incomes.
elimination_hint: National income = Net National Product at factor cost = money value of final goods and services produced — option D is the most accurate standard definition.
factual_hooks: National income = NNP at factor cost
Avoids double counting by counting only final goods
Measured using value-added, expenditure, or income approaches
contrast_with: GDP vs NNP vs National Income
contemporary_relevance: India revised its national income series to 2011-12 base year.

subject: Economy
trap_factor: NABARD is the apex body for rural credit but does NOT provide direct credit to rural households — it refinances banks. Only RRBs and Land Development Banks give direct credit.
elimination_hint: NABARD provides indirect credit (refinancing and supervision), not direct credit to households. RRBs and Land Development Banks directly lend to rural borrowers — answer is C (1 and 3 only).
factual_hooks: NABARD is the apex development bank for agriculture — provides refinance, not direct credit
Regional Rural Banks (RRBs) directly lend to rural households
Land Development Banks provide long-term agricultural credit directly
contrast_with: Apex institutions (NABARD) vs retail lending institutions (RRBs)
contemporary_relevance: Agricultural credit delivery remains a challenge despite multiple institutional mechanisms.

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Statement 1 (prohibition of traffic/forced labour) is a Fundamental Right (Article 23), NOT a DPSP — students confuse FRs with DPSPs. Only statement 2 (prohibition of intoxicating drinks) is a DPSP (Article 47).
elimination_hint: Article 23 (prohibition of human trafficking) is a Fundamental Right; Article 47 (prohibition of intoxicating drinks) is a DPSP — only statement 2 is in DPSPs.
factual_hooks: Article 23 (prohibition of trafficking) is a Fundamental Right
Article 47 (prohibition of intoxicating drinks for health) is a DPSP
DPSPs are non-justiciable but fundamental in governance
prerequisite_concepts: Part III vs Part IV of Constitution
related_concepts: Minerva Mills case (balance of FRs and DPSPs)
contrast_with: Justiciable Fundamental Rights vs non-justiciable DPSPs
contemporary_relevance: Courts have increasingly read DPSPs into Fundamental Rights expanding Article 21.

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Statement 1 says 'not more than 25 Members of Lok Sabha' — the actual composition includes up to 15 from Lok Sabha and up to 7 from Rajya Sabha (total 22). Statement 1 is factually wrong.
elimination_hint: PAC scrutinises appropriation/finance accounts and CAG reports — statements 2 and 3 are correct. Statement 1's '25 Lok Sabha members' is wrong; PAC has 22 members total.
factual_hooks: Public Accounts Committee has 22 members — 15 from Lok Sabha, 7 from Rajya Sabha
It scrutinises CAG reports and appropriation/finance accounts of government
PAC chairman is traditionally from the Opposition
contrast_with: PAC vs Estimates Committee vs Committee on Public Undertakings
contemporary_relevance: PAC is a critical instrument of parliamentary financial oversight.

subject: History
trap_factor: Dadu Dayal was born c.1544 (post-Babur) and Tyagaraja lived 1767-1847 — only Guru Nanak (1469-1539) was actively preaching when Babur defeated Ibrahim Lodi in 1526.
elimination_hint: Babur defeated Ibrahim Lodi at First Battle of Panipat in 1526. Only Guru Nanak (active 1499-1539) was preaching at that time; Dadu Dayal was born later, Tyagaraja was born centuries later.
factual_hooks: Babur defeated Ibrahim Lodi in 1526 (First Battle of Panipat)
Guru Nanak (1469-1539) was actively preaching during Lodi-Babur transition
Dadu Dayal was born ~1544; Tyagaraja born 1767 — both post-Babur
contrast_with: Northern Bhakti saints vs Southern saints (Tyagaraja — Carnatic music tradition)

subject: Environment
factual_hooks: Sikkim was declared India's first fully organic state in 2016
Organic farming eliminates synthetic pesticides and fertilisers
Sikkim won the Future Policy Gold Award 2018 from FAO
contrast_with: Organic farming vs conventional farming — economic and ecological trade-offs
mains_intro: Sikkim's transition to fully organic agriculture represents a pioneering model of sustainable development balancing ecological preservation with economic opportunity.
mains_body_points: Ecological benefits: elimination of chemical runoff protecting Himalayan rivers and biodiversity, improved soil health and carbon sequestration, reduction in pesticide-related health risks
Economic benefits: premium prices for organic products in domestic and international markets, growth in agro-tourism, improved farmer income through value-added organic certification
Challenges: initial yield dip during transition, market access limitations, need for organic input supply chains
Replicability: Sikkim model has inspired other states and gained international recognition
mains_conclusion: Sikkim's organic state achievement demonstrates that ecological sustainability and economic viability are complementary rather than competing goals in agriculture.
contemporary_relevance: Sikkim's model is increasingly cited in global discussions on sustainable food systems and agro-biodiversity.

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: Bandung and Yalta conferences are frequently tested — Bandung (1955) was the Afro-Asian conference, Yalta (1945) was WWII Allied leaders meeting. Bretton Woods (1944) set up IBRD and IMF.
elimination_hint: Bretton Woods Conference (1944, New Hampshire USA) established IBRD, IMF — note that GATT came from General Agreement in 1947, not Bretton Woods directly, but the question lists it as part of the agreements.
factual_hooks: Bretton Woods Conference (1944) established IBRD (World Bank) and IMF
It took place in Bretton Woods, New Hampshire, USA
GATT was a separate agreement signed in 1947 but linked to Bretton Woods framework
contrast_with: Bretton Woods vs Bandung Conference vs Yalta Conference
contemporary_relevance: Reform of Bretton Woods institutions to reflect emerging economy weight is an ongoing debate.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: NCCS (National Centre for Cell Science) is in Pune, not Mysore — this is a common error. CDFD is in Hyderabad and NBRC is in Manesar (Gurgaon), making pairs 2 and 3 correct.
elimination_hint: NCCS is in Pune (not Mysore) so pair 1 is wrong. CDFD is in Hyderabad (correct) and NBRC is in Manesar/Gurgaon (correct) — answer is C (2 and 3 only).
factual_hooks: National Centre for Cell Science (NCCS) is in Pune, Maharashtra
Centre for DNA Fingerprinting and Diagnostics (CDFD) is in Hyderabad
National Brain Research Centre (NBRC) is in Manesar, Haryana

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: BRIT sounds like it could be IT or road transport based on the acronym — it stands for Board of Radiation & Isotope Technology under DAE.
elimination_hint: BRIT (Board of Radiation and Isotope Technology) is a Government of India enterprise under Department of Atomic Energy engaged in isotope technology — production and supply of radioisotopes.
factual_hooks: BRIT = Board of Radiation and Isotope Technology under DAE
Produces radioisotopes for medical, agricultural, and industrial uses
Operates under Department of Atomic Energy (DAE)
contrast_with: BRIT vs BARC vs NPCIL in nuclear ecosystem
contemporary_relevance: Radioisotope production is essential for nuclear medicine and cancer treatment.

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Viruses are not decomposers — they are not living organisms in the traditional sense and do not break down organic matter. Only fungi and bacteria act as true decomposers.
elimination_hint: Decomposers break down complex organic matter into simpler inorganic compounds. Viruses don't metabolise or decompose; they are not decomposers. Answer is B (Fungi and Bacteria only).
factual_hooks: Decomposers are mainly bacteria and fungi
They convert complex organic matter to simple inorganic nutrients
Viruses are obligate parasites, not decomposers
contrast_with: Decomposers vs detritivores vs scavengers

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Students may choose warm-cold atmospheric currents or freshwater river outflow — but the key factor is where warm and cold oceanic currents meet, creating upwelling rich in nutrients.
elimination_hint: Where warm and cold ocean currents meet, upwelling brings nutrient-rich deep water to surface supporting massive plankton growth and fish populations — Grand Banks, Benguela, etc.
factual_hooks: Major fishing grounds are where warm and cold ocean currents meet (e.g., Grand Banks, Dogger Bank)
Upwelling zones bring nutrient-rich deep water to surface
Examples: Labrador + Gulf Stream, Humboldt Current zones
contrast_with: Ocean current convergence vs atmospheric current interactions
contemporary_relevance: Global fishing grounds are under stress from overfishing and ocean warming.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Statement 2 says rocket carries oxygen in gas form — rockets actually carry liquid oxygen (LOX) or solid oxidisers, not necessarily in gas form. Statement 1 is correct; statement 2 has an error about form.
elimination_hint: Statement 1 is correct: jet engines use atmospheric oxygen (unsuitable for space). Statement 2 is partially wrong: rockets carry oxygen but typically as liquid, not gas. Answer is A (1 only).
factual_hooks: Jet engines are air-breathing and cannot work in space (no atmosphere)
Rockets are self-contained: carry both fuel and oxidiser (liquid O2 or solid oxidisers)
Liquid oxygen (LOX) is the standard oxidiser in liquid-fuel rockets, not gas
contrast_with: Air-breathing engines vs rocket engines
contemporary_relevance: India's ISRO uses cryogenic engines (liquid H2 + liquid O2) in GSLV rockets.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Students may guess gas laser (as in older technologies) — semiconductor diode lasers are used in modern laser printers due to their compact size, efficiency, and precise wavelength.
elimination_hint: Semiconductor diode lasers (typically 780nm wavelength) are used in laser printers as they are compact, energy-efficient, and easily modulated for printing.
factual_hooks: Laser printers use semiconductor (diode) lasers
780nm wavelength is typical for laser printer diodes
Semiconductor lasers are also used in CD/DVD players and optical fibre communication
contrast_with: Semiconductor laser vs gas laser vs excimer laser applications

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Hydrogen and oxygen explosion sounds intuitive but it's less common in mines. Mine explosions are caused by methane (firedamp) mixing with air in coal mines.
elimination_hint: Methane (firedamp) naturally seeps in coal mines; when it mixes with air in certain proportions (5-15%), it becomes explosive — this is the most common cause of mine explosions.
factual_hooks: Methane + air (5-15% methane) is explosive in mines — called firedamp
Coal seams naturally release methane
Mine safety measures include ventilation and methane detection
contrast_with: Firedamp (methane) vs blackdamp (CO2) in mines
contemporary_relevance: Mine safety standards and methane capture for energy use are important policy areas.

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Private remittances are a current account item (transfers), NOT capital account — students often include them in capital account. Foreign loans, FDI, and portfolio investment are capital account items.
elimination_hint: Capital Account = Foreign Loans + FDI + Portfolio Investment + External Commercial Borrowings. Private Remittances go to Current Account (transfer account). Answer is B (1, 2, 4).
factual_hooks: Capital Account comprises FDI, portfolio investment, foreign loans, and banking capital
Remittances are Current Account items (private transfers)
India has one of the world's largest current account remittance inflows
contrast_with: Current account (trade, services, transfers) vs Capital account (investments, loans)
contemporary_relevance: India consistently receives the highest remittances globally, supporting its current account.

subject: History
trap_factor: Sanchi Stupa is famous for its sculptures/gateways (toranas), NOT mural paintings. Ajanta and Lepakshi are known for murals — students may mistakenly include Sanchi.
elimination_hint: Ajanta has famous Buddhist murals (2nd century BCE–6th century CE); Lepakshi (Vijayanagara era) has ceiling murals; Sanchi is famous for stone sculpture/toranas, not murals. Answer is B.
factual_hooks: Ajanta Caves have UNESCO-listed Buddhist murals from 2nd century BCE to 6th century CE
Lepakshi temple (Andhra Pradesh) has 16th century Vijayanagara mural paintings on ceiling
Sanchi Stupa is famous for sculptural toranas (gateways), not mural paintings
contrast_with: Mural painting sites vs sculptural sites in ancient India

subject: History
trap_factor: Students may confuse Calcutta (later sessions) with Bombay — the first session of INC (1885) was held in Bombay (now Mumbai) under W.C. Bonnerjee's presidency.
elimination_hint: First INC session (December 1885) was held in Bombay (Gokuldas Tejpal Sanskrit College) — a fundamental fact of Indian nationalism.
factual_hooks: First INC session held in Bombay (December 1885)
Presided by Womesh Chandra Bonnerjee
A.O. Hume was the founder; it met at Gokuldas Tejpal Sanskrit College
contrast_with: First session (Bombay 1885) vs first Calcutta session (1886, second INC)

subject: History
trap_factor: Statement 1 says Sankhya doesn't accept rebirth — this is wrong; Sankhya does accept transmigration. Only statement 2 (self-knowledge leads to liberation) is correct.
elimination_hint: Sankhya is dualistic — Purusha (spirit) and Prakriti (matter). It accepts transmigration and holds that discriminative knowledge (Viveka) between Purusha and Prakriti leads to liberation — statement 2 correct.
factual_hooks: Sankhya is one of six orthodox Hindu philosophical schools (Astika darshanas)
It accepts rebirth/transmigration as part of its metaphysical framework
Liberation in Sankhya comes from self-knowledge (distinguishing Purusha from Prakriti)
contrast_with: Sankhya (dualist, no God) vs Vedanta (monist, with Brahman)

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Statement 3 says 'Cabinet is headed by Head of State (President)' — this is wrong; Cabinet is headed by PM (Head of Government), not President (Head of State).
elimination_hint: In parliamentary democracy: Cabinet members must be MPs (statement 1 correct), ministers resign if they lose Parliament's confidence (statement 2 correct), but Cabinet is headed by PM not President — statement 3 wrong.
factual_hooks: Parliamentary government requires Cabinet members to be Parliament members
Collective responsibility means Cabinet falls if Parliament withdraws confidence
PM heads the Cabinet, not the President (who is a constitutional head)
contrast_with: Parliamentary (PM heads Cabinet) vs Presidential (President heads executive)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Francis Collins led the Human Genome Project (HGP) completion at NIH — students may confuse 'mapping' with Craig Venter's rival effort. All three pairs are actually correct.
elimination_hint: All three pairs are correctly matched: Francis Collins headed NIH's HGP; Sergey Brin co-founded Google; Jimmy Wales co-founded Wikipedia. Answer is D.
factual_hooks: Francis Collins directed the Human Genome Project at NIH (completed 2003)
Sergey Brin and Larry Page founded Google in 1998
Jimmy Wales and Larry Sanger co-founded Wikipedia in 2001
contrast_with: Public HGP (Collins) vs private genome sequencing (Venter/Celera)

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Students may include altitude differences (option 2) or strong winds (option 3) — but only the differential heating capacity of land vs water (continentality) explains the high annual temperature range in continental interiors.
elimination_hint: The sole reason for higher annual temperature range in continental interiors is the differential specific heat of land vs water — land heats and cools faster. Answer is A (reason 1 only).
factual_hooks: Land has lower specific heat than water — heats/cools faster (continentality)
Coastal areas are moderated by ocean's high specific heat capacity
Annual temperature range is highest in Siberia (continental interior) and lowest near oceans
contrast_with: Continental climate (extreme temperature range) vs maritime climate (moderate)

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Statement 3 (low ash fusion temperature) is wrong for Indian coal — Indian coal actually has HIGH ash content but HIGH ash fusion temperature making it unsuitable for certain industrial uses. Only statements 1 and 2 are correct.
elimination_hint: Indian coal characteristics: high ash content (30-45%), low sulphur content (<0.5%), and high ash fusion temperature — statement 3 is incorrect. Answer is A (1 and 2 only).
factual_hooks: Indian coal has high ash content (30-45%) — a major quality limitation
Indian coal has low sulphur content making it cleaner in that respect
High ash fusion temperature means Indian coal is not suitable for some metallurgical uses
contrast_with: Indian coal (high ash, low sulphur) vs imported coal (low ash, higher sulphur)
contemporary_relevance: India's high-ash coal raises environmental concerns and efficiency issues in thermal power plants.

subject: Economy
factual_hooks: Protectionism raises barriers to Indian exports — textiles, steel, pharmaceuticals hit hardest
Currency manipulation by trading partners distorts India's export competitiveness
India's trade deficit widens if partners erect barriers while India keeps markets open
contrast_with: Free trade vs protectionism impact on developing vs developed economies
mains_intro: Rising global protectionism and deliberate currency manipulation by major economies pose significant threats to India's macroeconomic stability through trade channel disruptions.
mains_body_points: Protectionism: Higher tariffs/NTBs in US, EU reduce demand for Indian exports (IT, textiles, pharma) — compresses export earnings, widens current account deficit
Currency manipulation: When trading partners artificially depress their currencies, Indian exports become uncompetitive; Indian firms face cheaper imports undermining domestic industry
Capital flows: Trade uncertainty reduces FDI and FPI inflows; currency volatility makes rupee vulnerable
Macroeconomic consequences: widening CAD, inflationary pressure through import cost rise, RBI forced to intervene depleting forex reserves
India's response levers: WTO dispute resolution, bilateral agreements, promoting domestic manufacturing (Make in India), diversifying export destinations
mains_conclusion: India must build export resilience through product and market diversification while engaging multilaterally to preserve rule-based trade order.
mains_interlinkages: GS3: Indian economy and external sector
contemporary_relevance: US tariffs under Trump 2.0 era and China's currency management directly threaten India's macroeconomic stability.

subject: Economy
trap_factor: The term 'S&P 500' could mislead students into thinking of supercomputers or digital technology given the '500' — it is simply the Standard & Poor's index of 500 large-cap US companies.
elimination_hint: S&P 500 (Standard & Poor's 500) is a benchmark stock market index tracking 500 large-cap US-listed companies — one of the most widely tracked equity indices globally.
factual_hooks: S&P 500 is a market-capitalisation-weighted index of 500 large US companies
It is a key barometer of US economic health
Managed by S&P Dow Jones Indices
contrast_with: S&P 500 (broad market) vs Dow Jones (30 stocks) vs NASDAQ (tech-heavy)
contemporary_relevance: S&P 500 movements influence global capital flows including FII inflows into India.

subject: Governance
trap_factor: This is a very specific factual question from 2008 about a government institution's location — students may guess Karnataka or Kerala; the institute was set up in Maharashtra.
elimination_hint: Rajiv Gandhi National Flying Training Institute is in Maharashtra (Gondia district) — a very specific factual question that requires direct recall.
factual_hooks: Rajiv Gandhi National Flying Training Institute is in Gondia, Maharashtra
It was established to train commercial pilots
Maharashtra hosts several aviation training institutions

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Students may confuse 10^6 (million) nanometres with 10^7 — 1 foot = ~30.48 cm = ~0.3048 m = 304,800,000 nm; 6 feet = 1,828,800,000 nm ≈ 183 × 10^7 nm.
elimination_hint: 1 meter = 10^9 nanometres. 6 feet ≈ 1.83 meters = 1.83 × 10^9 nm = 183 × 10^7 nm — answer is C.
factual_hooks: 1 nanometre = 10^-9 metres
1 metre = 10^9 nanometres
contrast_with: Nanometre vs micrometre vs millimetre

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Students may think the car's overall weight doesn't change so reactions remain the same — but the engine (usually at front) adds weight to front wheels, and in motion, weight distribution changes dynamically.
elimination_hint: At rest with only driver: weight is distributed equally. When car moves, aerodynamic and dynamic effects shift weight. But the key is the engine weight at front increases front wheel load even at rest vs moving; in motion, aerodynamic lift reduces front load slightly. Answer is B (less than R).
factual_hooks: Car weight distribution shifts when in motion due to aerodynamic forces
Engine weight at front increases static front wheel reaction
Dynamic effects during motion reduce front wheel normal force slightly
contrast_with: Static vs dynamic weight distribution in vehicles

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Statement 2 says laterite soils are rich in nitrogen and potash — they are actually poor in nitrogen and potash (leached away). Statement 3 is wrong — laterite soils are found in Kerala, Karnataka, Odisha, not Rajasthan/UP.
elimination_hint: Laterite soils are red (iron-rich), poor in N and potash, found in humid tropics (Kerala, Odisha, parts of Maharashtra/Karnataka), and support tapioca/cashew. Only statements 1 and 4 are correct.
factual_hooks: Laterite soils are red due to high iron/aluminium oxides
They are deficient in nitrogen, potash, lime — highly leached
Found in areas with heavy seasonal rainfall — Kerala, Odisha, Karnataka hills
Tapioca and cashew nuts grow well on laterite soils
contrast_with: Laterite soils (poor fertility, heavy rainfall areas) vs black cotton soils (high fertility, rain-shadow)

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Gondwana beds are associated with coal, not natural gas. Dharwars are ancient crystalline rocks associated with iron ore, not petroleum. Only mica in Kodarma (Jharkhand) is correct.
elimination_hint: Only statement 2 is correct: Kodarma (Jharkhand) is famous for mica mining. Natural gas is not in Gondwana beds (coal is); Dharwars are metamorphic/igneous rocks, not petroleum-bearing.
factual_hooks: Kodarma district (Jharkhand) is India's largest mica producer
Gondwana beds are coal-bearing formations, not natural gas
Dharwar rocks are Precambrian crystalline rocks — not petroleum-bearing
contrast_with: Gondwana (coal) vs Tertiary deposits (petroleum) in India

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Chambal and Narmada are both rivers in central India — students may confuse the Omkareshwar Project with the Chambal valley projects.
elimination_hint: Omkareshwar Dam is on the Narmada River in Madhya Pradesh — part of the Narmada Valley Development Project. Chambal has a separate set of multipurpose projects.
factual_hooks: Omkareshwar Project is on Narmada River, Madhya Pradesh
It is part of the Narmada Valley Development Authority projects
The dam is near Mandhata island at Omkareshwar (sacred site)
contrast_with: Narmada basin projects vs Chambal valley projects

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Wheat is a Rabi (winter) crop — students may include it as Kharif. Cotton, groundnut, and rice are all Kharif crops sown in June-July with monsoon onset.
elimination_hint: Kharif = monsoon season crops (June-Sept): cotton, groundnut, rice, maize, sorghum. Wheat is a Rabi (winter) crop. Answer is C (cotton, groundnut, rice — items 1, 2, 3).
factual_hooks: Kharif crops: rice, cotton, groundnut, maize, jowar, bajra — sown with monsoon
Rabi crops: wheat, mustard, gram — sown in winter (Oct-Nov)
Wheat is definitively Rabi, not Kharif
prerequisite_concepts: Kharif vs Rabi vs Zaid crop seasons
contrast_with: Kharif (monsoon) vs Rabi (winter) vs Zaid (summer) crops
contemporary_relevance: Kharif crop production is directly tied to monsoon performance — key for food security.

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Statement 1 says collective responsibility is to Parliament — actually it is to Lok Sabha (lower house), not Parliament as a whole. Statement 3 says PM communicates proposals for 'legislation' — Article 78 says PM communicates proposals for 'consideration of the Council of Ministers', not legislation.
elimination_hint: Council of Ministers is collectively responsible to Lok Sabha (Art. 75), not Parliament. Ministers hold office at President's pleasure (Art. 75). PM's Article 78 duty is to communicate Council decisions to President, not specifically legislation. Only statement 2 is fully correct.
factual_hooks: Article 75: Collective responsibility to Lok Sabha (not Parliament)
Article 75(2): Ministers hold office during President's pleasure
Article 78: PM's duty to communicate proposals considered by Council of Ministers to President
contrast_with: Collective responsibility (to Lok Sabha) vs individual responsibility

subject: Economy
factual_hooks: National Horticulture Mission launched in 2005-06 as Centrally Sponsored Scheme
Subsumed under Mission for Integrated Development of Horticulture (MIDH) in 2014-15
India is world's second largest producer of fruits and vegetables
contrast_with: NHM achievements vs persistent farmer income challenges
mains_intro: The National Horticulture Mission (NHM) was launched to holistically develop the horticulture sector through area expansion, productivity improvement, and post-harvest management.
mains_body_points: Achievements: India's horticulture production crossed 330 million tonnes, surpassing foodgrain production; area under horticulture expanded significantly
NHM supported creation of nurseries, demonstration farms, protected cultivation (greenhouses), and post-harvest infrastructure
Productivity: yield per hectare improved for several crops including potato, tomato, banana through better varieties and practices
Income impact: limited success — price volatility, poor post-harvest management, inadequate market linkages continue to erode farmer gains despite production increase
Shortcomings: inadequate cold chain, lack of processing facilities, middlemen exploitation, and poor access to market intelligence limit actual income realisation
mains_conclusion: While NHM successfully boosted production and area, translating this into sustained farmer income gains requires stronger market infrastructure, price stabilisation mechanisms, and value chain development.
contemporary_relevance: Doubling farmer income target (by 2022, now extended) depends critically on horticulture sector performance and market reforms.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Assertion is true (liver helps fat digestion via bile). The Reason is false — liver produces bile (not fat-digesting enzymes directly); actual fat-digesting enzymes (lipases) come from the pancreas.
elimination_hint: A is true (liver is important in fat digestion — produces bile salts that emulsify fats). R is false (liver does not produce fat-digesting enzymes; pancreas does via pancreatic lipase). Answer is C.
factual_hooks: Liver produces bile — emulsifies fats for digestion
Lipase (fat-digesting enzyme) is produced by pancreas, not liver
Bile salts break fat into smaller droplets for lipase action
contrast_with: Liver functions (bile production) vs pancreas functions (enzyme production)

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Statement 1 says NDC is an 'organ of Planning Commission' — NDC was actually a separate body that included Planning Commission members but was not subordinate to it. Statement 3 about Panchayats and economic plans is from Article 243G.
elimination_hint: Statement 1 is wrong: NDC was not an organ of Planning Commission; both were separate. Statement 2 (Economic & Social Planning in Concurrent List, Entry 20) is correct. Statement 3 (Art. 243G assigns planning to Panchayats) is correct. Answer is B.
factual_hooks: Entry 20 of Concurrent List: Economic and Social Planning
Article 243G: Panchayats shall prepare economic development and social justice plans
NDC was an independent body comprising PM, Union Ministers, CMs — not subordinate to Planning Commission
related_concepts: State list, Union list, Concurrent list
contrast_with: NDC vs Planning Commission — different constitutional status

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Statement 1 says Chairman and Deputy Chairman are NOT members of Rajya Sabha — this is wrong; the Vice President is Chairman of RS but is NOT a member, however the Deputy Chairman IS a member of Rajya Sabha. Statement 1 is partially wrong.
elimination_hint: Vice President (Chairman of RS) is NOT a member of RS; but Deputy Chairman IS elected from among RS members. Statement 1 is incorrect as stated. Statement 2 (nominated members vote in VP election) is correct. Answer is B.
factual_hooks: Vice President is ex-officio Chairman of Rajya Sabha but NOT its member
Deputy Chairman of Rajya Sabha IS an elected member of the House
Nominated members of Parliament can vote in VP election but NOT in Presidential election
contrast_with: VP election (all MPs including nominated) vs Presidential election (elected MPs and MLAs only)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: The assertion that 'radio waves bend in a magnetic field' sounds true because charged particles do — but radio waves are uncharged electromagnetic waves and do NOT bend in a magnetic field.
elimination_hint: Radio waves are electromagnetic (uncharged) — they do NOT bend in magnetic fields (only charged particles do). The assertion is FALSE, the reason is TRUE but doesn't explain the false assertion. Answer is D.
factual_hooks: Radio waves are electromagnetic radiation — they travel at speed of light
Magnetic fields deflect CHARGED particles, not uncharged electromagnetic waves
Only ionised particles or conducting media affect radio wave propagation
prerequisite_concepts: Magnetic force on charges vs waves
contrast_with: Charged particle deflection in B-field vs electromagnetic wave propagation

subject: Society
trap_factor: Karbi is from Assam (not HP); Bonda is from Odisha (not Tamil Nadu). Only Limboo (Sikkim) and Dongaria Kondh (Odisha) are correctly matched — pairs 1 and 3.
elimination_hint: Karbi tribe is from Assam's Karbi Anglong district, not Himachal Pradesh. Bonda tribe is from Malkangiri district of Odisha, not Tamil Nadu. Only pairs 1 and 3 are correct.
factual_hooks: Limboo (Limbu) is a Scheduled Tribe of Sikkim
Dongaria Kondh is a Particularly Vulnerable Tribal Group (PVTG) from Odisha (Niyamgiri Hills)
Karbi tribe belongs to Assam's Karbi Anglong; Bonda tribe is from Odisha
contrast_with: PVTGs vs Scheduled Tribes — special vulnerability criteria
contemporary_relevance: Dongaria Kondh's fight against Vedanta mining in Niyamgiri Hills is a landmark case in tribal rights and forest rights.
